MOTION TO DISMISS
Off Calendar Oct-17-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Nicholas Graves (402.871.5610; correspondinggraves@gmail.com) Plaintiff in propria persona.
Andrew Kislik, Esq. (415.762..3100; andy@njfirm.com) of Nassiri & Jung LLP for Defendants via Zoom VideoConference.

The Court noted there was no compliance with tentative ruling rule. Mr. Kislik stated his office misunderstood the instructions on the tentative ruling. The Court allowed oral arguments.

ARGUED The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS ADELAIDE HOSTEL, LLC, DAKOTA HOTEL, LLC and THE FITZGERALD HOTEL, LLC'S MOTION TO DISMISS.

Defendants' motion to dismiss under CCP 583.310 and 583.360 is denied. This wage-and-hour case was filed February 6, 2017. Thus, with the six-month extension of Covid-19 emergency rule 10, the CCP 583.310 period ran on August 7, 2022. However, as pro per plaintiff Nicholas Graves points out, defendants' July 13, 2022 case management conference statement insisted that trial could not begin because "[d]epositions have not been completed." (Opp. 4:9-16; 7/13/22 CMC Stmt. p. 2.) Defendants also stated that their "attorneys will not be available for trial" for certain dates in October 2022 and January 2023. (Id.) No mention was made of CCP 583.310 or 583.360 or the August 7 deadline.

The above estops defendants from seeking dismissal after "lull[ing] plaintiff into a false sense of security." (Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) 11-93; see also Borglund v. Bombardier, Ltd. (1981) 121 Cal.App.3d 276, 280-81.) Jordan v. Superstar Sandcars (2010) 182 Cal.App.4th 1416, 1422-23 is not to the contrary as plaintiffs there knew the date the five-year statute would run.

Defendant's reply brief argues they are not estopped because "Plaintiff was not led to believe that Defendants were waiving the 5-year statute, as he was unaware there was one." (Id. at 4:14-15.) In other words, defendants were fully aware of the five-year statute and knew the pro per plaintiff was not, yet made no mention of the statute in their CMC statement with less than a month to the deadline and instead insisted depositions must be completed before trial could start (while also giving unavailability dates after the deadline). This case calls for equity.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/RBU)
